description: Receptor type separation model for autism and epilepsy: hypothesis that autism-predominant presentations involve glutamatergic dysfunction while epilepsy-predominant presentations involve GABAergic dysfunction, with some genes involved in both conditions

autism_predominant.description: Genes primarily implicated in autism through glutamatergic ionotropic receptor dysfunction

genes.full_name: Glutamate ionotropic receptor kainate type subunit 1
Glutamate ionotropic receptor kainate type subunit 2
Glutamate ionotropic receptor AMPA type subunit 4
Glutamate ionotropic receptor delta type subunit 2

evidence.significance: Multiple rare variants in glutamate receptor genes show enrichment in autism cases vs controls

epilepsy_predominant.description: Genes primarily implicated in developmental and genetic epilepsies through GABAergic inhibitory dysfunction

genes.full_name: Gamma-aminobutyric acid type A receptor subunit beta3
genes.evidence_type: de novo mutations, loss of function

genes.full_name: Gamma-aminobutyric acid type A receptor subunit gamma2
Gamma-aminobutyric acid type A receptor subunit alpha1
Gamma-aminobutyric acid type A receptor subunit beta2

evidence.primary_variant_type: de novo loss of function mutations
evidence.significance: Strong burden of de novo variants in GABA-A receptor genes in developmental and genetic epilepsy cohorts

comorbid.description: Genes involved in both autism and epilepsy, with evidence of convergent dysfunction in ion channels and synaptic proteins

genes.function: Voltage-gated sodium channel, action potential initiation
sources: Developmental and Epileptic Encephalopathies and autism studies

genes.full_name: Potassium voltage-gated channel subfamily B member 1

evidence_epilepsy.type: de novo mutations and protein truncating variants

sources: Developmental and Epileptic Encephalopathies and autism studies

genes.full_name: Glutamate ionotropic receptor NMDA type subunit 2A
sources: Developmental and Epileptic Encephalopathies and autism studies

data_sources.name: CHOOSE Study
data_sources.description: Large autism case-control study with whole exome sequencing showing OR=45.7 enrichment for ionotropic glutamate receptor variants

data_sources.name: Developmental and Epileptic Encephalopathies Studies
data_sources.description: Multiple published studies of developmental and epileptic encephalopathy cohorts showing de novo variants in GABA receptor and ion channel genes

data_sources.name: Autism Convergence Studies
data_sources.description: Studies showing evidence of convergent dysfunction in comorbid genes across autism cohorts

cell_type_evidence.source: Sanders/Nowakowski BA22 2026 (Suresh, Wigdor, Hao et al.)
cell_type_evidence.description: Single-nucleus multiomics of BA22 speech cortex in 100 donors provides cell-type resolution for receptor framework

findings.excitatory_neurons_most_affected: L4/5 IT neurons show strongest DEG burden and chromatin accessibility changes across all comparisons. Deep-layer excitatory neurons are the primary cellular substrate.
findings.inhibitory_neuron_concordance: Genome-wide log2FC correlation between genetically diagnosed and undiagnosed ASD is strongest in inhibitory neurons, suggesting GABAergic populations carry the most concordant signal across genetic backgrounds.
findings.downregulated_pathways: Mitochondrial, oxidative phosphorylation, ATP metabolic, ribosomal processes (P < 1e-20)
findings.upregulated_pathways: Neuronal projection development, dendrite morphogenesis, synapse organization, cell junction assembly, small GTPase signaling (P < 1e-9)
findings.rfx3_regulatory_hub: RFX3 motif accessibility increased across excitatory neuron subtypes. RFX3 regulates IEGs via CREB/CREBBP. IEGs enriched but paradoxically downregulated (compensatory pattern).

verbal_nonverbal_dimension.description: Verbal vs non-verbal differences within ASD cohort map to specific neuron types and regulatory programs
verbal_nonverbal_dimension.sample_size_caveat: Small Ns (~20-26 donors with verbal phenotype data). Interpret cautiously.

findings.rfx3_pattern: Coordinated differences in RFX3 RNA expression, motif accessibility, and eRegulon activity between verbal and non-verbal individuals. Cell-type dependent.
findings.evolutionary_enrichment: Regulatory regions associated with verbal/non-verbal differences enriched in human-gained enhancers (HGEs) and primate-conserved regions linked to language.
findings.evidence_tier: C -- descriptive, small sample, not replicated

diagnosed_vs_undiagnosed.description: Same biology, different effect sizes across genetic backgrounds

findings.genome_wide_correlation: Moderate Spearman rho between NDD/ASD and ASD-only log2FCs using split controls (1000 iterations). Strongest in inhibitory neurons.
findings.interpretation: Consistent with spectrum from high-penetrance rare variants to distributed common risk converging on same neuronal programs at different magnitudes.

interpretation.key_insight: The receptor type separation model proposes that dissociable biological pathways underlie autism vs epilepsy phenotypes: glutamatergic excitatory dysfunction in autism-predominant presentations, GABAergic inhibitory dysfunction in epilepsy-predominant presentations, with shared ion channel genes involved in both conditions. Sanders BA22 2026 adds cell-type resolution: excitatory L4/5 IT neurons are most affected, and the diagnosed-vs-undiagnosed comparison shows same biology at different effect sizes.
interpretation.clinical_implications: This framework may help stratify patients into mechanistically distinct groups for targeted therapeutic approaches: GABA-enhancing or glutamate-reducing strategies for epilepsy-predominant, GABA-reducing or glutamate-enhancing for autism-predominant. The verbal/non-verbal dimension adds a language-specific axis linked to RFX3 regulatory activity in specific neuron types.
interpretation.research_priorities: Functional validation of rare variants in glutamate and GABA receptor genes; cell-type specific expression analysis; electrophysiological characterization of variant effects; translational studies in patient-derived neurons; replication of verbal/non-verbal RFX3 finding in independent cohorts; investigation of RFX3-CREB-IEG pathway in autism models
